documents.title: Alexithymia Awareness Network
documents.text: Alexithymia Awareness Network. Difficulty identifying or describing emotions can be isolating. Start with plain-language answers, careful research, and support options you can check for yourself.

documents.title: Frequently Asked Questions
documents.text: Jump to a question What is alexithymia? Is alexithymia a mental disorder? Do people with alexithymia have emotions? How common is alexithymia? Why do I feel blank, numb, confused, or “not normal” emotionally? Can alexithymia affect relationships, love, care, or empathy? How can someone explain alexithymia to another person? Why can body sensations be clear when emotion words are not? Is alexithymia related to autism, ADHD, trauma, depression, anxiety, or dissociation? What helps with alexithymia? How can I find support or a therapist who understands this? Can alexithymia change over time? Where can I learn more or connect with others? What is alexithymia? Alexithymia is a personality construct involving difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings to others, and a more externally oriented style of thinking. In everyday terms, a person may know that something is happeni

documents.title: Diagnosis & Assessment
documents.text: Alexithymia is usually treated in the research literature as a dimensional construct, not as a stand alone clinical diagnosis. In practice, this means assessment tools can help describe patterns in emotional awareness and communication, but a score by itself does not diagnose a person (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997; Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). Use this for orientation. Bring personal mental health, trauma, medication, or treatment questions to a qualified clinician. Short answer There is no simple checklist that can diagnose alexithymia. Questionnaires and interviews are measurement tools. They need interpretation in context. Self screening can be useful for reflection or for preparing questions, but it is not a clinical conclusion. A clinician may also consider mood, anxiety, trauma, autism, ADHD, medical symptoms, substance use, language/culture, and other factors that can affect emotional awareness. What assessment can and cannot answer Assessment can help describe patterns such as difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings, or a more externally focused thinking style. It can also help researchers compare groups or track change over time. Assessme

documents.title: Assessment Instruments
documents.text: Use instruments to ask better questions, not to self diagnose. Important boundaries Alexithymia is usually treated in the literature as a dimensional construct, not a stand alone diagnosis. Self report scores depend on the person, setting, language, current state, and purpose of assessment. Cutoffs used in research should not be treated as personal clinical conclusions. A high score can be a reason to ask better questions, not proof of what someone has or needs. Diagnosis, treatment, medication, trauma, severe symptoms, or safety questions belong with qualified clinicians. For a broader assessment overview, see Diagnosis & Assessment. Common instruments TAS 20 The 20 item Toronto Alexithymia Scale (TAS 20) is one of the most widely used self report alexithymia measures. Key AAN citations include the original TAS 20 item selection and cross validation paper, a later TAS 20 review, and a meta analytic confirmatory factor analysis review (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 1994; Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 2020; Schroeders, Kubera, and Gnambs, 2022). AAN describes TAS 20 scores as screening or research measurements, not diagnoses. Detailed scoring, cutoffs, language versions, and clinical inte

documents.title: Therapy Approaches
documents.text: Use this to prepare better clinician questions. Short answer The review sources support caution: studies are heterogeneous, and the literature does not identify one standardized therapy protocol for alexithymia (Pinna et al., 2020; Tsubaki and Shimizu, 2024). What can be said safely The safest public statement is limited: alexithymia may matter for assessment, communication, treatment response, and progress tracking; some psychological treatment studies report reduced TAS 20 scores, but the review literature is not strong enough to standardize a protocol; personal treatment questions should be handled with qualified clinicians who can consider safety, co occurring conditions, history, and goals. Do not infer Do not infer that CBT, ACT, DBT, psychodynamic therapy, mindfulness, EMDR, somatic therapy, group therapy, or any other named approach is proven to treat alexithymia. Also do not infer that therapy cannot help or that one style fits everyone. Questions to bring to a clinician Bring questions like these: How do you work when a client cannot quickly identify or describe feelings? How would we track progress without relying only on emotion labels? Are trauma, dissociation, autism,

documents.title: Related Conditions
documents.text: Alexithymia is a personality construct, not a diagnosis. It can be studied alongside many medical, psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and stress related conditions, but a co occurrence finding does not tell an individual person what they have, why they have it, or what treatment they need. What “co occurrence” means here In research and clinical writing, “co occurrence” usually means that two conditions or traits are observed together more often than expected, or are being discussed together in the same person or group. For AAN, that needs several cautions: alexithymia itself is usually measured as a dimensional trait, commonly with self report instruments, not diagnosed as a standalone disorder; a co occurrence rate depends on who was studied, which alexithymia measure was used, and what cutoff was chosen; overlap does not prove cause in either direction; emotional awareness difficulties can also be shaped by stress, trauma, depression, anxiety, autism, physical illness, culture, language, medication, and many other factors. For general definitions and assessment caveats, see the FAQ. Autism and alexithymia A systematic review and meta analysis by Kinnaird, Stewart, and Tchanturia (

documents.title: Treatment
documents.text: Use this as background for clinician conversations, not as a treatment plan. Evidence check Do not treat any therapy, medication, app, exercise, or modality as proven treatment for alexithymia itself. Two review sources matter here: Pinna, Manchia, Paribello, and Carpiniello reviewed alexithymia and treatment response across psychiatric disorders and described a complex, mixed evidence base rather than a single treatment rule (Pinna et al., 2020). Tsubaki and Shimizu reviewed psychological treatment studies and reported decreases in TAS 20 scores in many included studies, while emphasizing heterogeneity, possible bias, and the need for larger and more consistent randomized trials before interventions can be standardized (Tsubaki and Shimizu, 2024). Enough for caution. Not enough to rank therapies, promise improvement, or give self treatment instructions. Do not infer Do not infer that: alexithymia has a standard cure; a specific therapy modality is proven to treat alexithymia; a medication treats alexithymia itself; a questionnaire score determines what care someone needs; treatment evidence from one population applies to every person; AAN can tell a reader what treatment to choose

documents.title: Emotion Frameworks
documents.text: Emotion science does not have one settled theory of what emotions are. Different frameworks ask different questions: some focus on evolution, some on brain and body regulation, some on appraisal and meaning, some on dimensions such as valence and arousal, and some on practical vocabulary. Use this as a map, not a verdict. A framework can help reflection or education without proving the final structure of emotional life. Why disagreement matters Researchers do not only disagree about details; they sometimes disagree about the basic unit being explained. Izard described continuing disagreement about how to define emotion while defending a distinction between basic emotions and broader emotion schemas (Izard, 2007). Barrett and colleagues later argued that basic emotion theory and constructed emotion theory rest on incompatible assumptions and should not be blended into a tidy compromise (Barrett et al., 2025). For AAN, the practical lesson is to keep levels separate: a vocabulary tool is not the same thing as a brain theory; a clinical or self reflection prompt is not the same thing as a diagnosis; a theory may be useful at one level of analysis and contested at another; different mo

documents.title: Lexicon: Words for Feelings
documents.text: This lexicon is a starting aid, not a complete map of emotion science. It is for people who want more words to compare, reject, adapt, or bring into conversation. Alexithymia is commonly described as difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings, and a more externally oriented thinking style (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021; Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997). A word list cannot diagnose alexithymia, prove what someone is feeling, or replace clinical support. It can sometimes make reflection less blank. Using the word list Use these words as prompts, not answers. 1. Start with what is easiest to notice: the situation, body cues, action urges, energy level, or whether something feels pleasant, unpleasant, mixed, or unclear. 2. Pick a broad word first. “Unpleasant,” “activated,” “flat,” “tense,” or “not sure” is a valid starting point. 3. Try two or three nearby words instead of forcing one exact label. 4. If no emotion word fits, record the facts: what happened, what your body did, what you wanted to do, and what changed afterward. 5. For personal mental health, trauma, medication, diagnosis, treatment, or immediate safety questions, consult qualified support outs

documents.title: Resources
documents.text: Research, tools, support, and reading resources.

documents.title: Digital Tools & Apps
documents.text: AAN is not recommending a specific app for alexithymia. Boundaries Apps and digital tools should not be presented as: alexithymia diagnostic tools; treatment for alexithymia; replacements for therapy, medical care, immediate safety support, or qualified assessment; reliable authorities on what a person is feeling; evidence that a person has or does not have a mental health condition. Alexithymia is discussed here as a research construct and measurement topic, not as a stand alone public diagnosis or app detectable condition (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). Tool categories Some people use digital tools for: emotion vocabulary or feeling wheel prompts; mood, activity, symptom, or body sensation tracking; journaling; mindfulness, grounding, or body scan practice; AI assisted journaling or emotion label suggestions. Category names are not recommendations. A tool can be well designed for reflection and still be inappropriate for diagnosis, treatment decisions, immediate safety needs, or sensitive data. AI tools need extra caution Treat AI emotion labels as guesses, not facts. Before entering sensitive information, check: whether labels are optional, uncertain, and correctable; what

documents.title: Books and Reading Lists
documents.text: Read for background. Do not use books as diagnosis, treatment, or personal care instructions. Academic sources Source Why it may matter Public note Taylor, Bagby, and Parker (1997), Disorders of affect regulation: Alexithymia in medical and psychiatric illness Academic book connected to alexithymia history, assessment, and medical/psychiatric framing. Use as scholarly background, not personal clinical guidance. Cambridge Handbook of Alexithymia (2024 excerpt) Handbook level orientation that may help readers locate definitions, assessment topics, autism, treatment, and clinical context. A citation pointer/excerpt is available; readers should consult the source directly for details. Reading checks Before trusting a claim, ask: Does the author cite peer reviewed alexithymia research? Are claims about diagnosis, prevalence, treatment, or outcomes clearly sourced? Is the book about alexithymia specifically, or about a nearby topic such as emotion vocabulary, mindfulness, trauma, philosophy, or well being? Does the source acknowledge uncertainty and differences between people?

documents.title: Conferences & Events
documents.text: Conferences, webinars, symposiums, and community events can help readers follow current work on alexithymia, emotional awareness, interoception, support, and related clinical or research topics. Before relying on an event listing Use the official event page when checking whether an event is current, relevant, and accessible. the official event name and link; dates, location, and whether online attendance is available; the organizing society or host organization; submission, registration, or accessibility notes when relevant and verified; a plain language reason the event may matter for alexithymia, emotion research, support, education, or related clinical/research communities. Read cautiously Treat event descriptions as invitations to check details, not as endorsements. Be careful with: standing conference names without a current official event page; stale calendars from earlier years; claims that an event is “best,” “recommended,” or clinically useful without evidence; sponsor style language or copied promotional descriptions; broad category lists that do not explain why an event is relevant.

documents.title: Research Participation Studies
documents.text: Research participation studies are different from published research papers. A paper is a record of completed work; a study opportunity asks people to volunteer time, information, data, or biological samples for current research. Before considering participation Use the official study page, university page, clinical trials registry, or lab contact information before deciding whether an opportunity is real and current. Check whether the study is active or recruiting. Confirm the institution, research team, sponsor, and contact details. Read eligibility criteria, location or remote participation requirements, time commitment, compensation, and privacy terms. Look for ethics, consent, or institutional review information when applicable. Be cautious of any study description that promises diagnosis, treatment, or a personal explanation for alexithymia. Why caution matters Research opportunities can close, change eligibility rules, or move to a new contact link. A stale listing may waste a reader's time or create false confidence about a study's purpose. Important note AAN does not run these studies unless a listing explicitly says so. Participation decisions are personal and voluntary.

documents.title: Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions
documents.text: Plutchik's wheel is one historical emotion vocabulary framework, not a settled map of how emotions work and not evidence about alexithymia diagnosis or treatment. Primary source: Robert Plutchik's The Emotions (Plutchik, 1980). What can be said from the cited source Plutchik proposed a psychoevolutionary theory of emotion and a wheel like vocabulary structure. The framework is commonly associated with eight primary emotion labels: joy, trust, fear, surprise, sadness, disgust, anger, and anticipation (Plutchik, 1980). The framework can serve as a reference point for emotion vocabulary. It may help a reader compare broad labels, intensity words, or possible mixtures, but that is a practical use of a model, not proof that emotions always have exactly these categories or combinations. Do not infer Do not infer that: Plutchik's wheel is a diagnostic instrument; the wheel can identify what a person is really feeling; the wheel measures alexithymia; using the wheel treats alexithymia; all cultures or individuals organize emotions according to this model; every derivative wheel image online is accurate, authorized, or appropriate for AAN to reproduce. Use the framework carefully Use Plutch

documents.title: Gloria Willcox and the Feeling Wheel
documents.text: Gloria Willcox is the author credited in the publication metadata for The Feeling Wheel . Why Willcox appears on AAN Willcox appears here because of The Feeling Wheel , a short 1982 article in the Transactional Analysis Journal (Willcox, 1982). The Feeling Wheel is widely recognizable as a practical emotion vocabulary aid: it arranges feeling words in a visual structure so a person can move from broad categories toward more specific words. For AAN readers, the safest public framing is modest: a feeling wheel can be a reflection prompt or vocabulary scaffold. It should not be treated as a diagnosis, a measure of alexithymia, a treatment, or proof that emotions have one fixed structure. Relevance to alexithymia Alexithymia is often described in the literature as involving difficulty identifying feelings and difficulty describing feelings to other people. A vocabulary tool may help some people compare possible words, notice uncertainty, or bring examples to a clinician or trusted supporter. That does not mean a person is "supposed" to find the right word on the wheel. Some people may need to start with body sensations, events, behavior, pleasant/unpleasant feeling, activated/calm feel

documents.title: Paul Ekman
documents.text: Paul Ekman is often cited in discussions of facial expression, basic emotion theory, and emotion recognition. Use this as emotion framework context, not alexithymia authority, diagnosis, or treatment guidance. Why Ekman appears on AAN Ekman's work is relevant because many public emotion vocabulary tools use a small set of basic emotion labels such as anger, disgust, fear, happiness, sadness, and surprise. For some readers, a concrete list of emotion words can be a useful starting point for reflection. That usefulness is limited. A basic emotion list is not a test of what someone is feeling, and it should not be used to infer a person's inner state from their face. Alexithymia is about difficulty identifying and describing one's own feelings; facial expression frameworks are only one adjacent way people have tried to make emotion more observable. What the basic emotion account claims In Ekman's classic statement of the view, basic emotions are argued to be evolved responses with partly distinctive features, including signals, physiology, antecedent events, and action tendencies (Ekman, 1992). Earlier work associated with Ekman helped popularize the idea that some facial expressions

documents.title: Peter E. Sifneos
documents.text: Peter E. Sifneos is included here because his 1973 paper, “The prevalence of ‘alexithymic’ characteristics in psychosomatic patients,” is the source AAN cites for the term entering the research literature (Sifneos, 1973). Why this paper matters Sifneos’s 1973 paper gave researchers and clinicians a name for a pattern that later alexithymia literature continued to study: difficulty identifying and describing feelings, often discussed alongside an externally oriented thinking style (Luminet, Nielson, and Ridout, 2021). Modern AAN pages treat alexithymia as a dimensional construct, not as a diagnosis or a fixed identity category. The original context was psychosomatic medicine. That history is important, but it should not be read as saying that alexithymia causes physical illness, that bodily symptoms are “just emotional,” or that one therapy approach is automatically appropriate for everyone. Historical source Sifneos authored the 1973 paper cited here as foundational to the alexithymia term. The paper appeared in Psychotherapy and Psychosomatics in 1973 (citation record). Later alexithymia research built out assessment tools, construct debates, and clinical questions beyond the orig

documents.title: Graeme J. Taylor
documents.text: Why Taylor appears on AAN Graeme J. Taylor is important to AAN's alexithymia source trail because his name appears on two recurring reference points: the 1997 book Disorders of Affect Regulation: Alexithymia in Medical and Psychiatric Illness , co authored with R. Michael Bagby and James D. A. Parker (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997); the 1994 TAS 20 development paper, co authored with R. Michael Bagby and James D. A. Parker (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 1994). The relevant point for readers is historical and bibliographic: Taylor's work is part of the published literature around alexithymia assessment, especially the Toronto Alexithymia Scale 20 (TAS 20), and the affect regulation framing used in alexithymia research. TAS 20 context The TAS 20 is a self report measurement instrument used in alexithymia research. AAN describes it as a screening or research measure, not as a diagnosis by itself. Scores should be interpreted carefully, especially outside research settings or without professional context. Do not infer This page does not establish a current affiliation, grants list, leadership summary, or career history. Do not infer diagnosis or treatment from a TAS 20 score. Bring pers

documents.title: R. Michael Bagby
documents.text: Why Bagby appears on AAN R. Michael Bagby is included here because his name appears on several recurring AAN source trails for alexithymia assessment and affect regulation research: the 1994 TAS 20 item selection and cross validation paper, co authored with James D. A. Parker and Graeme J. Taylor (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 1994); the 1997 book Disorders of Affect Regulation: Alexithymia in Medical and Psychiatric Illness , co authored with Graeme J. Taylor and James D. A. Parker (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997); the 2006 Toronto Structured Interview for Alexithymia paper, co authored with Taylor, Parker, and Susan E. Dickens (Bagby, Taylor, Parker, and Dickens, 2006); a 2020 review marking twenty five years of the TAS 20 literature, co authored with Parker and Taylor (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 2020). The relevant point for readers is bibliographic and historical: Bagby's published work is part of the literature around the Toronto Alexithymia Scale 20 (TAS 20), the Toronto Structured Interview for Alexithymia (TSIA), and affect regulation framing in alexithymia research. Public profile evidence A public ORCID record for R. Michael Bagby confirms the name form but does not provide

documents.title: Harrie C. M. Vorst
documents.text: Harrie C. M. Vorst is listed, with Bob Bermond, as an author of the 2001 validation paper for the Bermond–Vorst Alexithymia Questionnaire (BVAQ), a self report alexithymia measure used in research (Vorst and Bermond, 2001). BVAQ relevance The BVAQ is an assessment instrument, not a diagnosis. It is one research measure among several alexithymia instruments, and its validation paper is part of the literature behind alexithymia measurement. Use BVAQ claims cautiously: It is a self report questionnaire used in alexithymia research. It is associated with cognitive and affective dimensions in the BVAQ literature. It should not be used by itself to diagnose a person or replace professional assessment. Detailed scoring, cutoffs, translations, and clinical use claims should come from qualified sources in an appropriate setting. Name note The BVAQ validation paper lists the name form “Harrie C. M. Vorst.” Other name variants should not be treated as public biographical claims without their own sources. Read next FAQ — basic measurement and diagnosis boundary caveats. Research papers — selected literature records. Resources — AAN resource hub. For personal assessment or treatment questions,

documents.title: Support
documents.text: Find directories, peer spaces, and practical supports. Professional directories Check fit directly with any provider: credentials, scope, availability, cost, privacy, telehealth options, and emergency coverage policies. Psychology Today therapist directory — psychologytoday.com/us/therapists TherapyDen therapist directory — therapyden.com Inclusive Therapists — inclusivetherapists.com Open Path Collective — openpathcollective.org Bring treatment questions to a qualified clinician. For background, read Treatment and Therapy Approaches. Community and peer spaces Before posting, check privacy, moderation, rules, and current activity. r/Alexithymia — reddit.com/r/Alexithymia AAN hosted forum — alexithymia.us/forum Practical supports For workplace, school, family, relationship, disability, legal, or accommodation questions: gather concrete examples, then use the qualified support for that set

documents.title: Mission
documents.text: The Alexithymia Awareness Network (AAN) is an informational, community oriented project about alexithymia. Our work is meant to help people find plain language explanations, credible research, and supportive next steps without treating a website as a clinician. What AAN is here to do Increase awareness of alexithymia in clear, non stigmatizing language. Make information easier to find by connecting readers with cited explainers, research papers, support resources, and practical learning paths. Respect lived experience while keeping public claims modest, accurate, and separate from personal medical advice. Support further research and learning by preserving references, identifying evidence gaps, and improving how alexithymia resources are organized. Build community carefully by making it easier for people with alexithymia, families, clinicians, researchers, and allies to orient themselves and ask better questions. What AAN does not do AAN does not diagnose alexithymia, provide therapy, prescribe treatment, or replace qualified care. Screening tools and educational pages can be useful starting points, but personal questions about mental health, assessment, or treatment belong with qu

documents.title: Who AAN is for
documents.text: The Alexithymia Awareness Network is an informational and community oriented project for people trying to understand alexithymia, and for people who support, study, teach, or work with them. AAN offers education and contact pathways. It is not a clinic, emergency service, membership association, or referral network. Who may find AAN useful AAN is intended for several overlapping audiences: people who relate to alexithymia or are wondering whether the concept fits their experience; family members, partners, friends, and supporters who want clearer language for conversations; clinicians, educators, coaches, and peer supporters looking for plain language orientation and source backed references; researchers, students, writers, and journalists looking for a careful starting point; organizations or policy audiences trying to understand why emotional awareness, communication, and support access matter. People arrive with different needs. Some are looking for vocabulary, some for research, some for support options, and some for ways to explain alexithymia without shame or overstatement. What “network” means here “Network” means a public orientation and contact point around AAN’s mission.

documents.title: Advisors
documents.text: Researchers, clinicians, educators, community organizers, and people with relevant lived experience can help AAN keep public information careful, humane, and useful. Ways to contribute expertise If you would like to support AAN's educational work, please email team@alexithymiaawarenessnetwork.org. Related pages Resources — public resource hub Support — urgent, professional, community, and practical support routes

documents.title: Contact
documents.text: Use this address for non urgent AAN contact. Email: team@alexithymiaawarenessnetwork.org What to email about You can use this address for: questions or feedback about AAN pages; research, clinician, media, or collaboration inquiries; brief personal experience notes that may help AAN understand community needs. What email cannot do AAN is informational and community oriented. We cannot provide diagnosis, treatment, therapy, immediate safety support, or personal medical advice by email. Please avoid sending urgent safety concerns or highly sensitive medical details through this inbox. If your question is about your own mental health, assessment, or care options, a qualified clinician is the right person to consult. Safety and clinical limits Please do not use the AAN inbox for immediate safety, medical, or emergency needs. AAN is not equipped for that role. For support pointers, see Support.

documents.title: Bob Bermond
documents.text: Bob Bermond is listed, with Harrie C. M. Vorst, as an author of the 2001 validation paper for the Bermond–Vorst Alexithymia Questionnaire (BVAQ), a self report alexithymia measure used in research (Vorst and Bermond, 2001). BVAQ relevance The BVAQ is an assessment instrument, not a diagnosis. It is one research measure among several alexithymia instruments, and its validation paper is part of the literature behind alexithymia measurement. Use BVAQ claims cautiously: It is a self report questionnaire used in alexithymia research. It is associated with cognitive and affective dimensions in the BVAQ literature. It should not be used by itself to diagnose a person or replace professional assessment. Detailed scoring, cutoffs, translations, subtype claims, and clinical use claims should come from qualified sources in an appropriate setting. Name and biography note The BVAQ validation paper lists the name form “Bob Bermond.” This page does not establish a current institutional profile, full biography, or broader professional role claim. Read next FAQ — basic measurement and diagnosis boundary caveats. Research papers — selected literature records. Resources — AAN resource hub. Harrie C.

documents.title: James D. A. Parker
documents.text: Why Parker appears on AAN James D. A. Parker is included here because his name appears on several recurring AAN source trails for alexithymia assessment and affect regulation research: the 1994 TAS 20 item selection and cross validation paper, co authored with R. Michael Bagby and Graeme J. Taylor (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 1994); the 1997 book Disorders of Affect Regulation: Alexithymia in Medical and Psychiatric Illness , co authored with Graeme J. Taylor and R. Michael Bagby (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker, 1997); the 2006 Toronto Structured Interview for Alexithymia paper, co authored with Bagby, Taylor, and Susan E. Dickens (Bagby, Taylor, Parker, and Dickens, 2006); a 2020 review marking twenty five years of the TAS 20 literature, co authored with Bagby and Taylor (Bagby, Parker, and Taylor, 2020). The relevant point for readers is bibliographic and historical: Parker's published work is part of the literature around the Toronto Alexithymia Scale 20 (TAS 20), the Toronto Structured Interview for Alexithymia (TSIA), and affect regulation framing in alexithymia research.
